# ARC-QUIC-INT-0004 - QUIC Interop Post-Handshake Stream Open/Accept Architecture

## Purpose

Describe the smallest managed child-process sequencing boundary that proves the client can open the first application stream and the server can accept it after handshake completion without claiming transfer or retry support.

## Scope

This slice covers the managed child-process testcase that proves the first application stream can be opened after the existing handshake completes and accepted by the peer on the same real path. It does not claim transfer enablement, byte delivery, or EOF.

## Requirements Satisfied

- REQ-QUIC-INT-0011

## Design Summary

The managed client/listener hosts already own the honest handshake completion boundary. This architecture does not widen that boundary. Instead, it records the child-process sequencing that happens after it: the new `post-handshake-stream` testcase reuses the real managed bootstrap path, the client opens one outbound application stream only after connect completes, and the server accepts one inbound application stream only after accept completes.

The runtime already owns the active-phase stream-open and inbound-accept mechanics, so the harness only has to drive the real path and prove the first open/accept pair happened after handshake completion. No byte delivery, EOF, transfer, retry, or broader 1-RTT/TLS policy claim is added.

## Key Components

- src/Incursa.Quic.InteropHarness/InteropHarnessRunner.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic.InteropHarness/InteropHarnessEnvironment.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicConnection.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicConnectionRuntime.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicClientConnectionHost.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicListenerHost.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicTlsTransportBridgeDriver.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicTlsKeySchedule.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/QUIC/REQ-QUIC-API-0004.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/CRT/REQ-QUIC-CRT-0121.cs

## Data and State Considerations

The harness only coordinates process lifecycle, testcase dispatch, and post-handshake sequencing. The library remains responsible for connection state, stream registry, and 1-RTT readiness. The client opener path depends on the existing client-role 1-RTT readiness gate, and the server accepter path depends on the existing active-phase accept gate.

## Edge Cases and Constraints

- Handshake completion must precede the first stream open and accept.
- The slice remains child-process only and single-stream only.
- The harness must not claim file delivery, EOF, multi-stream, retry, 0-RTT, key update, or broader 1-RTT readiness.
- transfer and retry stay unsupported outside this slice.

## Alternatives Considered

- Treat the slice as the transfer contract and require byte delivery. Rejected because that widens the boundary beyond the smaller prerequisite the runtime already supports.
- Add a synthetic test-only bypass that opens or accepts streams without the real managed child-process harness path. Rejected because it would not prove the actual production seam.

## Risks

- A later change could accidentally widen the test into byte-delivery or EOF proof and turn this prerequisite into a transfer claim.
- A synthetic bypass would hide the actual child-process sequencing gap rather than proving the real seam.
- The runtime already owns the open and accept primitives, so the remaining risk is in harness sequencing and proof discipline, not in inventing new protocol behavior.

## Boundary Split

Harness-owned code stays limited to `InteropHarnessRunner.cs`, process lifecycle, testcase dispatch, and child-process proof.
Library-owned code keeps `QuicConnection`, `QuicConnectionRuntime`, `QuicClientConnectionHost`, `QuicListenerHost`, and the stream open/accept mechanics.
